COOLBOY, or CASTLEBAY, a village in the p. of Carnew, co. Wicklow, 21 m. S of Tinehely. Pop. in 1831, 105.
COOLCASHIN, a parish in co. Kilkenny, 31 m. NW of Freshford. Pop. 455.

COOLE, a parish in co. Cork, 31 m. SE of Fermoy. Area 1,153 acres. Pop. 283.

COOLE, a village in the p. of Mayne, co. Westmeath, 31 m. WNW of Castle-Pollard. Pop. 371. COOLEAGH, CAMINEAGH, OF CUMMINEER, & mountain-pass a little above the source of the Lee, and on the new road from Macroom to Bantry, on the W border of co. Cork. This deep and extraordinary chasm, which, within the last ten years, has been taken advantage of in order to make a road between Macroom and Bantry, is one of the most picturesque things in Ireland. The proposed Berehaven line of railway traverses the pass.
COOLGRANEY, a village in the p. of Inch, co. Wexford, 31 m. SSW of Arklow. Pop. 311.
COOLINE, or COOLINEY, a parish in co. Cork, 21 m. SSW of Charleville. Area 1,152 acres. Pop. 437.
COOLING, a parish in Kent, 41 m. NNE of Rochester. Area 2,000 acres. Pop. 144.
COOLKERRY, a parish in Queen's co., 11 m. E by S of Rathdowney. Area 1,618 acres. Pop. 457.
COOLMUNDRY, a parish in co. Tipperary, 21 m. E of Fethard. Area 1,688 acres. Pop. 350.
COOLOCK, a parish, containing a village of the same name, 3 m. NE of Dublin, in co. Dublin. Area 1,734 acres. Pop. 947.

COOLOO, or Kulu, a mountain-district of N India. situated in the angle formed by the Sutledge and the Himalaya, between the states of Rampur and Comarsein. It has little cultivable soil. Its cap. is Sultanpore. Its raja was tributary to Runjit Singh before the overthrow of the Sikh state of Lahore.—Also a town in the prov. of Orissa, 80 m. SE of Sumbulpore .- Also a banza of Congo in SW Africa, on the r. bank of the Congo river, 15 m. from its mouth. Pop. 300. A fall in the river occurs at this place, where it has forced its way between bare syenitic rocks on both sides, and is divided into two narrow channels by a slaty island which rises 15 ft. above the ordinary level of the river, but appears to be nearly covered in the floods at the rainy season.
COOLRAINE, a village in the p. of Offerlane, Queen's co., 33 m. WSW of Mountrath. Pop. 193.
COOLSCAMP, a commune and town of Belgium, in the prov. of E Flanders, arrond. and 12 m. S of Bruges. Pop. 2,554. It has important manufactories of linen.

COOL-SPRING, a township in Mercer co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U. S., 175 m. WNW of Harrisburg. Pop. 1,934.—Also a township in Washington co., in North Carolina, 190 m. E of Raleigh.— Also a township in Wilkinson co., in Georgia, 56 m. SE of Milledgeville.—Also a township in Warren co., in Kentucky.—Also a township in La Fayette co., in Missouri.
COOLSTUFF, a parish in co. Wexford, 3 m. E by N of Taghmon. Area 3,347 acres. Pop. 636.

COOMASSIE, the capital of the African kingdom of Ashantee, in N lat. 6° 34' 50", W long. 2° 12'. [Bowdich.] It is built upon the side of a large rocky hill of iron-stone, and encircled by a dense forest. It is insulated by a marsh close to the town northwards, and by a narrow stream, 1 m. distant from it NW, and 60 yards broad; and close to it on the NE, E. SE, and S, and about 100, 20, 70, and 50 yards broad at these points. In many parts the depth of this stream state of Pennsylvania, U. S., 138 m. NE of Harris- after rains is 5 ft., but commonly 2 ft. The marsh contains many springs, and supplies the town with water; but exhalations from it cover the city with a thick fog morning and evening, and engender dysentery. The town is 4 m. in circumf., not including the suburbs of Assafoo and Bantama, which are m. distant, and were formerly connected by streets with the city, as was evident from the numerous ruins of houses on the path when Bowdich visited C. Four of the principal streets are a 1 m. long, and from 50 to 100 yards wide. "I observed them building one," says Bowdich, "and a line was stretched on each side to make it regular. The streets were all named, and superior captains in charge of each; ours, for instance, was Aperremsoo, i.e. 'Big gun' or 'Cannon street,' because those taken when Dankara was conquered were placed on a mound at the top of it. The area in which we had our first audience was called Daëbrim, i. e. 'the Great market,' in distinction to a lower street called Gwaba, or 'the Small market.' The street above where we lived was called Osamarandiduüm, meaning literally, 'with 1,000 muskets you could not fight those who live there." The palace is situated in a long wide street running through the middle of the town, from which it is shut out by a high wall, terminating at each end on the marsh. It included two or three small streets, besides several areas and piazzas, for the king's relief and recreation when the superstitions of the country confine him to the palace. This building consists of a variety of oblong courts and regular squares, the former presenting arcades along one side, some of round arches symmetrically turned, having a skeleton of bamboo; the architraves and bases are exuberantly adorned with bold fan and trellis-work of Egyptian character; they have a suite of rooms over them, with small windows of wooden lattice, of intricate but regular carved work, and some with frames of gold. The squares have a large apartment on each side, open in front, with two supporting pillars, which break the view, and give it all the appearance of a proscenium of the stage of the older Italian theatres; they are lofty and regular, with cornices of a very bold fan-work in alto relievo, a drop-curtain of curiously plaited cane is suspended in front, and in each is splendid furniture, such as chairs embossed with gold, stools and couches of rich silk, or scattered regalia. The most ornamented part of the palace is that appropriated to the women. Except two open door-ways, the fronts of some of these apartments are closed by panels of curious open carving, conveying a striking resemblance at first sight to a florid Gothic screen; one front is entirely closed, and had two curious doors of a low Saxon arch, and strengthened or battened with wood-work, carved in high relief, and painted red. A small grove at the back of the large market-place was called Sammonpomë, or 'the Spirit-house,' because the trunks of all the human victims were thrown into it. Several trees are scattered about the town; and small circular elevations of two steps, the lower about 20 ft. in circumf., like the bases of the old marketcrosses in England, are raised in the middle of several streets, on which the king's chair is placed when he goes to drink palm-wine there, his attendants encircling him. "The Ashantees," says Bowdich, "persisted that the pop. of C. when collected was upwards of 100,000. I think it likely to be much greater than that of Sego, -which Mr. Park reported as 30,000,from the extended masses of crowd I observed on festivals, when the plantations of the environs are almost wholly deserted. Perhaps the average resident pop. of C. is not more than 12,000 or 15,000." The markets are held daily, from eight o'clock in the morning until sunset. The larger contains about 60 stalls or sheds, formed of a small square frame covered with cotton cloth, with a pole from the centre, stuck into the ground, besides throngs of inferior venders seated in all directions. Amongst the articles for sale are beef and mutton cut in small pieces for soup; wild hog, deer, and monkey's flesh; fowls, pelts of skins, yams, plantains, corn, sugar-cane, rice, encruma, a mucilaginous vegetable richer than asparagus, which it resembles; peppers, vegetable butter, oranges, papaws, pine-apples, bananas, salt and dried | in S lat. 36° 40', E long. 149° 30'.—Also a summit of COO fish from the coast; large snails, smoke-dried and stuck in rows on small sticks in the form of herring bone; eggs for fetishes, palm-wine, rum, pipes, beads, looking-glasses, sandals, silk, cotton cloth, powder, small pillows, white and blue cotton thread, and ealabashes.—There are 9 great paths leading from C., viz., the Dwabin, Akim, Assin, Warsaw, Sanee, Gaman, Soko, Daboia, and Sallagha.
COOMBE, a township in the p. of Presteigne, Herefordshire, 2 m. ESE of Presteigne. Pop. 121.
COOMBE-BISSET, a parish in Wilts, 31 m. SW of Salisbury. Area 3,110 acres. Pop. 406.
COOMBE-KEYNES, a parish in Dorsetshire, 6 m. WSW of Wareham. Area 1,470 acres. Pop. 135.
COOMBS, a parish in Sussex, 2 m. SSE of Steyning. Pop. 80.
COOMBS-EDGE, a township in the p. of Chapelen-le-Frith, Derbyshire, 21 m. N of Buxton. Pop. 328.
COONDOOZ. See KUNDUZ.

COOPER, a river in South Carolina, U. S., which unites with Ashley river to form Charleston harbour. It is connected with Santee river by a canal 21 m. in length.—Also a county in the central part of Missouri. Area 400 sq. m. It is intersected by La Mine and Little Saline rivers. The cap. is Booneville. Pop. 10,484.—Also a township in Washington co., in the state of Maine, 196 m. ENE of Augusta. Pop. 657. -Also a township in Kalamazoo co., in Michigan, 131 m. W of Detroit. Pop. 376.

COOPER CREEK, a river in Central Australia, flowing between the meridians of 140° and 142° E, and nearly under the parallel of 27° 40'S. It comes from the NE. The late Mr. Kennedy was of opinion that this river is identical with the Victoria.

COOPER (PORT), a settlement and harbour on the E coast of the Middle island of New Zealand, on the N coast of Banks' peninsula. The port has a common entrance with Port-Ashley, 2 m. in width, a little to the E of the spot where the hills of the peninsula join the level land of the isthmus. It is exposed to the NE, but is easily accessible, except in strong SW winds. At low water, the head of the harbour presents a large mud flat of blue clay from 500 to 600 acres in extent, with a small stream of fresh water running through it. The hills on the E side of the entrance to the harbour present a rounded and down-like appearance, with a dense herbaceous vegetation; those on the W side are more precipitous and abrupt. The portion of the peninsula contiguous to Port C. consists of gently undulating downs covered with rich groves, and equalling the finest grazing-districts of New South Wales. See Canterbury.
COOPER'S, a village in Smith co., in the state of Tennessee, 60 m. E of Nashville.—Also a village in Franklin co., in the state of Virginia, 169 m. WSW of Richmond.
COOPERSBURG, a village in Lehigh co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U. S., 95 m. ENE of Harrisburg.

COOPER'S ISLAND, an island of the Antilles, in the Virgin group, to the SE of the island of Tortola, and between the islands of Ginger and Salt, in N lat. 18° 23', W long. 64° 29'. It is 5 m. in length, and about 1 m. in breadth. It belongs to the English.—Also a small island near the SE extremity of the isle of Georgia, in the South Atlantic ocean, in S lat. 54° 50', W long. 36°.
COOPER'S ISLAND, or KUIPER'S ISLAND, an islet in the vicinity of Batavia, on the N coast of Java, about a 1 m. S of the island of Onrust. It is the site of numerous warehouses.
COOPER'S MOUNT, a summit of SE Australia, Eyre Land, SW Australia, in S lat. 33° 2', E long. | 134° 43'.

COOPERSTOWN, a village of Oswego township and co., in the state of New York, U. S., 69 m. W of Albany. It is pleasantly situated at the outlet of Oswego lake, and contained in 1840 about 1,400 inhabitants.-Also a village, consisting of a few dwellings, in Plumb township, Venango co., in the state of Pennsylvania.
COORAKOO, a town of Sudan, in the district and 78 m. N of Jacoba.
COORG. See Kurg.

COOS, a county in the N part of the state of New Hampshire, U. S., comprising an area of 1,600 sq. m., bordering on Lower Canada on the N, and bounded on the W by Connecticut river. It is watered also by Mohawk, Ammonoosuck, Israel's, John's, and Androscoggin rivers; and its S part is intersected by the lofty range of the White mountains. Excepting on the banks of the streams it possesses little fertility. Pop. in 1830, 8,390; in 1840, 9,849. Its capital is Lancaster.

COOSA, a county in the easterly part of the state of Alabama, U. S., 870 sq. m. in extent, situated on the E side of a river of the same name, and drained by its affluents Hatchet, Paint, and Wawakee creeks. It is finely diversified, and is generally fertile. Pop. in 1840, 6,995. Its cap. is Rockford. The C. river takes its rise in Georgia, in the Cherokee district, under the name of the Etowah; enters the state of Alabama; runs along the E border of St. Clair, Shelby, and Autaugo counties; and on the SW of the latter, 4 m. below Wetumpka, unites with the Talapoosa river to form the Alabama. Its total course is 240 m., and it is navigable for steam-boats 8 m. above the junction to Wetumpka.
COOSAWDA, a village in Autaugo co., in the state of Alabama, U. S, 106 m. SE of Tuscaloosa, on the W bank of the Alabama.
COOSAWHATCHIE, a town, the cap. of Beaufort district, in S Carolina, U. S., 134 m. S of Columbia, on a river of the same name, which runs SSE, and falls into Broad river.

COOSIMA, a very small volcanic island in the archipelago of Japan, in N lat. 41° 21' 30", directly over against the strait of Sangar. It is a peaked, weather-beaten lava rock, of a dark blue colour, rising to the height of 900 ft. above sea-level, and smokes incessantly. About 6 m. to the NW is a similar island, called Oosima. The two islands lie directly in sight of the Japanese town of Matsa, or Matsmai. They are both uninhabited, and so naked that not even a pile of grass grows upon them.
COOSOO, a large town of W Africa, in the Yariba territory, 60 m. W by S of Katunga. It is a place of considerable traffic and transit commerce.

COOTOO, an open village of W Africa, in the Yariba territory, 16 m. NNE of Coosoo. Landes says the country to the S of this place has numerous blocks of granite scattered over its surface, with a good deal of wood; but to the N the soil becomes richer and deeper.
COOTEHALL, a village in co. Roscommon, on the Boyle river, 31 m. S by W of Battle-Bridge.

COOTEHILL, a post-town in the p. of Drumgoon, in co. Cavan, 71 m. NNW of Shircock, on a stream of the same name, which, rising in co. Monaghan, flows into the Annalee after a run of 18 m. The town is comparatively well-built; and its linen market is one of the briskest among the second-rate towns of Ireland. Pop. in 1831, 2,239; in 1841, 2,425. COPAIS, TOPOLAIS, or TOBOL (LAKE OF), an extensive sheet of water in N Greece, bounded on the N by the nomarchy of Locris; on the E and S COP by that of Thebes; and on the W by that of Bœotia; intersected by the parallel of 38° 25' N, and the meridian of 23° 5' E. It occupies an extensive plain, which becomes narrower to the NE, where it is continued into a level and open valley which is bounded at length by a chain of rocky mountains that include the water as if within a wall. The surface of the lake is estimated at 120,000 stromata = 30,000 acres. On the N and E sides of Parnassus, and on the S declivities of the Œta range, this lake has an immense reservoir whence the heavy rains of winter, and the melted snows of spring, descend into it. It is traversed, in its whole length, by the little river Cephissus. The mass of water which is here collected was, in ancient times, carried off naturally through the katabothra or fissures and cavities in the bounding limestone range. These were carefully kept open; but when the rich Orchomenus was plundered and destroyed by the Thebans, the cleansing of these channels was intermitted; and the channels of egress becoming gradually closed up, the noble plain on which, it is said, twelve cities containing 500,000 men once flourished, was gradually converted into a marsh covered with reeds and rushes, and the abode only of millions of fishes and frogs. The eels of the C. were anciently famous; and a fishery of them is still carried on at the katabothra. Strabo says: "A chasm or gulf, close to the lake, opened under ground a passage of about 30 stadia in length; the river was received into this, and then burst into view again." The Larmi rises with a large volume of water at about 4 m. distance from the lake. It is not improbable, therefore, that by this stream a considerable portion of the waters of the C. finds an outlet .- Fiedler, Reise durch König. Griech. in den Jahren 1834 bis 1837.—Raikes's Journal in Walpole's Memoirs relating to Turkey, &c., vol. i.

COPAKE, a township of Columbia co., in the state of New York, U. S., 49 m. SE of Albany. It is mountainous in the E; but towards the W there is a fine broad valley, watered by Ancrum creek. Pop. in 1840, 1,505.

COPAN, a ruined city of Guatemala, in the prov. of Honduras, district of Comayagua, on the r. or N bank of a stream of the same name, which flows NW into the Matagua, at a point 50 m. distant from the ruins, and 160 m. above the mouth of the Matagua in the bay of Honduras. The C. below this point is partly navigable for canoes during the rainy season, but falls impede the navigation before it joins the Metagua [Galindo]. Juarros says it formerly was "an opulent city, the court of the cacique Copan-Calel, the conquest of which cost Hernando de Chaves a great deal of trouble and fatigue. Francisco de Fuentes, who wrote the chronicles of this kingdom, assures us that in his time, that is in the year 1700, the Great circus of C. still remained entire. This was a circular space, surrounded by stone pyramids about 6 yds. high, and very well constructed; at the bases of these, were figures, both male and female, of very excellent sculpture, which then retained the colours they had been enamelled with; and, what was not less remarkable, the whole of them were habited in the Castilian costume! In the middle of this area, elevated above a flight of steps, was the place of sacrifice. The same author relates that, at a short distance from the circus. there was a portal constructed of stone, on the columns of which were the figures of men, likewise represented in Spanish habits, with hose, ruff round the neck, sword, cap, and short cloak! On entering the gateway, he adds, there are two fine stone pyramids, moderately large and lofty, from which is suspended a hammock containing two human figures, one of each sex, clothed in the Indian style. Astonishment is forcibly excited," Juarros continues, "on viewing this structure, because, large as it is, there is no appearance of the component parts being joined together; and, although entirely of stone, and of an enormous weight, it may be put in motion by the slightest impulse of the hand." The present extent of the ruins of C. along the S bank of the river, according to Stephens, is about 2 m., but how far they extend into the depths of the forest it has been impossible to ascertain. On the opposite bank, at a distance of about 1 m., a ruin was observed by Duplais on the top of a mountain 2,000 ft. high, which may have belonged to the city. Of palaces or other dwellings, there are no longer any remains in this place; but running along the river from N to S is a wall 624 ft. in length, and from 60 to 90 ft. high, forming one of the sides of an oblong enclosure, which it is customary to denominate the Temple, and the other three sides of which are formed by a succession of pyramidal structures and terraced walls measuring from 30 to 140 ft. in height. The river-wall is built of hewn-stones from 3 to 6 ft. in length, and 11 ft. in breadth. It is accessible from the river by flights of steps, similar flights leading on the inner side down into the enclosed area. At a short distance from the SW angle of the river-wall are two small pyramidal structures, one of which is connected with part of the citywall running along the l. bank of the river, and which seems to have flanked a gateway. Down the sides of the walls and pyramids, and covering the ground of the quadrangular enclosures, are innumerable remains of sculpture, some still maintaining their original position, others forming heaps of fragments, among which, however, many blocks are remarkably well preserved. Half-way up the sides of one pyramid are rows of Death's heads, of colossal proportions, but which, from their peculiar conformation, are supposed to represent the skulls of monkeys, and not of men-a supposition strengthened by the fact, that among the fragments at the foot of the pyramid was found the effigy of a colossal ape or baboon, bearing a strong resemblance to the animals of the same species originally figured on the great obelisk from the ruins of Thebes, now in the Place-de-la-Concorde in Paris. Among the fragments on the ground were also several human heads, sculptured, like those of the apes, in bold relief, and impressing the beholder with the belief that they were portraits. The whole of the sides of the terraced walls and pyramids have seemingly been decorated with similar sculptures fixed by stone tenons driven into the wall. In many cases—as remarked by earlier visitors—traces of colour are still visible, indicating that these sculptures had been painted. At the foot of one of the pyramidal walls, in the court-yard most distant from the river, stands one of the ornaments which form the peculiar characteris- 1,439. tics of the ruins of C. These are stone columns or obelisks, from 11 to 13 ft, in height, from 3 to 4 ft. in width, and something less in depth, having on the principal face a human figure sculptured in high relief, presenting its full front, with the upper part of the arm pressed close to the body, and the lower part brought forward so as to allow of the hands being pressed against the breast. They are all clad in rich garments—in Castilian fashion, Fuentes would assure us-some in the form of short tunics, others more like pantaloons. The feet, which are of clumsy form, are generally covered with a kind of buskin; and the heads are adorned with coverings of the most fanciful description. The idol-for such these objects are supposed to have been-particularly alluded to differs from others in its vicinity, inasmuch as it is broader at top than below, while the sculpture is in lower relief. The face is of a calm and placid expression, and the sculptured ornaments, though difficult to define, are graceful and pleasing in design. The back and sides of the monument are covered with hieroglyphics. In front of it is an altar 4 ft. high, and 6 ft. square, of one block of stone, resting on four globes cut out of the same material. The bas-reliefs on the sides represent a series of sixteen human figures, seated cross-legged in oriental fashion. Each bears in his hand a weapon, and the heads of all are covered with peculiar head-dresses without plumes. The top of the altar is divided into 36 tablets of hieroglyphics. From the pyramidal terrace forming the outer wall of the smaller court within the temple there is a subterraneous passage leading to the river-wall, and below this, a sepulchral vault was opened by Colonel Galindo, who explored the ruins in 1834 on account of the Mexican government. On each side of the vault, which is 6 ft. high and 10 ft. long by 5 ft. in width, are small niches, which, at the time of the opening, contained numerous earthenware vessels filled with human bones packed in lime. The floor of the vault, paved with stones, and coated with lime, was strewn with various articles, such as stone knives, stalactites, marine shells, and a small Death's head, cut or cast in a green stone, and described as of exquisite workmanship. In one only of the ruined cities of Central America have there been found monuments similar to the idols of C .- At some distance from the ruins, deep in the heart of the wide-spreading forest, are the quarries whence have been drawn the materials for all the buildings and monuments which exist here.—The modern C. is a small hamlet standing near the junction of the brook Sesesmil with the C. river, in the W suburb of the ancient city, in N lat. 14° 45', W long. 90° 52', according to Galindo.
COPANO, a newly founded town in Texas, near the N extremity of the bay of Aransaso. It has long been a landing-place for goods destined for the interior, and all points on the San Antonio river.
COPAY, one of the Hebrides, in Inverness-shire, to the W of the island of Harris.
COPDOCK, a parish of Suffolk, 3½ m. SW of Ipswich, on the London and Norwich railway. Area 610 acres. Pop. 299.
COPE (CAPE), a headland of Spain, in the prov. of Murcia, in N lat. 37° 25′, W long. 1° 32′.
COPEJA, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Para, which forms one of the eight branches of the Hiapura or Coqueta.
COPELAND ISLAND, a small wedge-shaped islet on the N coast of Australia, in S lat. 11° 28', E long. 132° 43'. COP

COPELAND ISLANDS, a cluster of isles off the S side of the entrance of Belfast lough, co. Down. The largest, called Big Island, lies 11 m. E by N of Nout-Head point, and comprises 230 acres of arable land, and 40 acres of rough ground. The sound between it and the mainland, though foul near the latter, has a depth of from 7 to 8 fath. The pop. about 10 years ago was 75.—Cross or Lighthouse island lies rather less than 1 m. NNE of Big island. It is about 1 m. in circumf. A light-house upon it, 52 ft. high to the lantern, is in N lat. 54° 41′ 44", W long. 5° 32'. A perennial spring bursts from a rock on this island, at a point about 60 ft. above the level of the sea.—Mew island, about 10 perches SE of Cross island, contains 7 or 8 acres of low pasture land. A great tide, commonly called the tide of Stranygore, and occasioned from a collision of tidal currents from the North channel and Belfast lough, runs off from the E extremity of the Copeland islands, to the NE and the Mull of Galloway.
COPELY, a township of Summit co., in the state of Ohio, 124 m. NE of Columbus. It has an undulating surface, and is generally fertile. Pop. in 1840,

COPENAME, or CUPANAMA, a river of Dutch Guayana, which takes its rise in the mountainous territory of the Guacanayas, runs N, inosculates with the Niqueri, and after a total course of 135 m. falls into the Atlantic at Batavia, 30 m. W of the embouchure of the Surinam. It varies from 3 m. to 6 m. in breadth, but is much obstructed by rocks and sand-banks. COPENHAGEN [Danish, Kiöbenhavn, i. e. 'the Merchant's harbour; Swed. Kopenhamn; Germ. Kopenhagen], the capital of Denmark, and the only city or town in the Danish dominions of any considerable magnitude, is situated partly on a small promontory on the E coast of the isle of Sieland, and partly on the adjacent island of Amak, at the S extremity of the Sound, in N lat. 55° 40′ 53", E long. 12° 34′ 20" [Conn. des Temps], N lat. 55° 40', E long. 12° 34' [Raper]. Though of no great extent, it is one of the handsomest cities in the N of Europe. It is between 4 and 5 m. in circumf.; and consists of three principal divisions; the Old and the New town, and Christianshavn. It is fortified towards the land with regular ramparts, and 24 bastions, and is environed with a wet ditch, both broad and deep. The ramparts are planted with a double row of lime-trees, and laid out in walks. Without the lines are the suburbs of North Bridge, East Bridge, and West Bridge. On the sea-side, the principal defence is the Crown battery, which is at the entrance of the port about a 1 m. from the shore. This work is built in the form of a square; the water flows into the middle of it; and, within the last thirty years, it has been greatly strengthened and enlarged. The citadel, which stands at the NE extremity of the city, is small, but formidable. The whole city is divided into 12 quarters; and contains 10 squares, 5 market-places, 3 palaces, 9 parish-churches, and 7 hospitals. The gradual rise of the shore behind the city is particularly favourable to the view of C. which seems to lie upon a wide-sloping garden; while the many fine spires, and other elevated objects in the city, give to it an appearance of splendour quite equal to that of Dublin seen from the bay [Inglis]. The streets are in general broad and well paved, with a footpath on each side; but, in some instances, are very narrow and inconvenient; some of them are intersected by canals, which afford great facility to the transportation of goods. The Rue-de-Goths, and Amalien- Gade in particular, are beautiful streets. The former is about \( \frac{3}{4} \) of a m. in length. There is a peculiarity in the mode of building in this city, which seems a judicious and convenient one in a crowded city. Instead of the usual right angles formed by the corners of the houses at the extremities or divisions of the streets, the builders of C. have squared them off in a semi-octangular form, and thereby secured various advantages. Carriages and horses cannot so frequently run foul of each other, or run down persons on foot, at the turnings of the streets; and the space gained gives a free circulation of air, and the appearance of as many handsome squares as there are street divisions in the city. Most of the houses are modern, and built of brick, sometimes stuccoed to resemble stone; a few are constructed of freestone brought from Germany. They are, in general, spacious, having four complete stories, besides sunk cellars and garrets; those of the nobility in particular are splendid, and elegantly finished in the Italian style of architecture. The shops are, as usual, confined to the ground story; but as they make no such prominent appearance as with us, they do not disfigure the rest of the building. The Oldstedt, or Old C., occupies the W division of the city, N of the harbour, and contains the principal public buildings. The palace of Christianborg, in this quarter, was originally erected by Christian VI. out of his own private purse, and is said to have cost 6,000,000 dollars. It was destroyed by fire in 1794; but has been rebuilt on its original scale. The front, which is of stone, is 367 ft. long; and the lateral sides, built of brick stuccoed, are 389 ft. The elevation is 114 ft., with 6 stories, of which 3 are upon a large scale. The principal suite of apartments is in the fourth story. The interior decorations of the original palace were equal to its external grandeur; but so sudden and rapid was the conflagration, that very little of its furniture and pictures were preserved. The apartments are at present but partially furnished. The Rittarsaal or 'knight's saloon,' was particularly splendid. It was 118 ft. by 58, with a gallery on each side richly gilded, and supported by 44 columns of cinnamon wood. It has been well restored, and is decorated with a richly sculptured frieze. One wing of this edifice is occupied by the supreme court of justice. The upper floor contains the royal collection of paintings. On the N side of the palace, but detached from it, is the museum of the great national sculptor Thorwaldsen, nearly 300 of whose works are collected here. The Kongens-Nytorv, or 'the King's new market,' is a spacious and irregular area situated nearly in the centre of this division of the city. Upwards of 12 of the principal streets concentrate in this market place. On one side is the castle of Charlottenburg, part of which is now appropriated to the Royal academy of paintings, architecture, and sculpture; and in the middle of the area, is an equestrian statue of Christian V. in bronze. The observatory, erected by Frederick V. for a disciple of Tycho Brahe, and forming the tower of Trinity church, is particularly deserving of attention. It is built in the form of a cylinder, about 70 ft. in diam., and is 115 ft. in height; and has a spiral carriage road of brick to within 20 or 25 ft. of the top. From the rooms where the astronomical apparatus is kept, there is a fine and extensive prospect. In this quarter of the city is a simple and elegant obelisk of Norwegian granite, situated without the walls near the W gate, on the road to the king's summer-palace of Fredericksborg, which was erected in honour of Christian in commemoration of his granting freedom to all the peasants on the crown-lands. The old town contains also the dock, the admiralty, the exchange, the university, and the royal colleges, besides several handsome churches and hospitals. The New town, or Friederickstadt, which was raised by Frederick V., is extremely beautiful; and consists chiefly of an octagon, called Frederick's Plads, and four broad streets leading to it in opposite directions. The grand entrance is through a gate composed of double rows of Corinthian pillars, with a rich entablature. The octagon contains four uniform and elegant palaces, with two wings each, forming what is called the Amalienborg; and in the centre is an equestrian statue of Frederick V. erected at the expense of the Danish East India company. Two of these palaces form the residence of the king; another is allotted to the different members of the royal family, and another to the prince royal. At the extremity of one of the streets is Frederick's church, which, though begun many years ago, and though large sums of money have been expended upon it, is left unfinished, and the materials still lie scattered over the church-yard. Its plan and style of architecture are grand and elegant. The walls, inside and outside, were all to be of polished Norwegian marble. The small Gothic palace of Rosenberg, said to have been built by Inigo Jones, stands near the rampart a little to the E of the N gate. It contains the state apartments, where the king holds his annual bed of justice; and its gardens, which are very extensive, are the principal promenade of the citizens. In this division of the city, are also Frederick's hospital, the citadel of Frederickhavn, and the esplanade. Christianshavn is built upon the island of Amak, to the S of the Old and New towns. It is connected with Old C. by a draw-bridge across the harbour; and contains the dock-yards, where ships-of-war are refitted, and the marine arsenal. The magazines, forges, and workshops, are all upon an excellent construction; and the rope-walks are each 1,000 ft. long. Ship-building, indeed, is nowhere better understood than at C., and its admirable harbour can also afford it advantages in this respect superior to those of almost any other city. The island of Amak is about 4 m. long, and 2 m. broad; and is chiefly peopled by the descendants of a colony from East Friesland, to whom this island was consigned by Christian II. at the request of his wife Elizabeth, sister of Charles V., for the purpose of supplying her with vegetables, cheese, and butter. From the intermarriages of these colonists with the Danes, the present inhabitants are chiefly descended; but as they wear their own dress, and enjoy peculiar privileges, they appear a distinct race from the natives. The inhabitants have their own inferior tribunals; but, in capital offences, are amenable to the king's court of justice at C. The old national habit, brought by the original colony when they first migrated to this island, is still in use amongst them. It resembles the habit of the ancient quakers, as represented in the pictures of the Dutch and Flemish painters. The men wear broad-rimmed hats, black jackets, and full, glazed breeches of the same colour, loose at the knees, and tied round the waist. The women are dressed chiefly in black jackets, and red petticoats, with a piece of blue glazed cloth bound on their heads. The island contains about six villages, and between 3,000 and 4,000 souls. The island is laid out in gardens and pastures; and still, according to the original design, supplies C. with milk, butter, and vegetables. The house-of-correction in this quarter of the city is under excellent management, and merits the attention of the philanthropist. The church of St. Saviour, with a tower 288 ft. high, the finest in the city, and the warehouses of the Danish East India company, are in this division.—The harbour of C. is formed by the straits of Kelleboe, which separate Sieland from Amak. It is capable of holding 500 ships, and lies completely within the fortifications of the town. The entrance is so narrow that only one ship can enter at a time; and it is protected by the cannon of the citadel, and several other batteries, of which the most formidable is that of the Three crowns. Ships-of-the-line are thus moored in the very heart of the city with their bowsprits rising above the windows of the houses; and merchantvessels are brought by the canals close to the warehouses that line the quays. Every ship-of-war has its particular station, with a separate store house on the water's edge opposite to where she is moored. C. is distinguished for its numerous public establishments, and charitable institutions. There are 22 hospitals, one of which accommodates above 2,000 patients, and 30 poor-houses. The principal literary societies of C. are, the Royal Academy of Sciences, instituted in 1743; the Royal Economical society, founded in 1768, whose object is to promote the fine arts, fisheries, agriculture, horticulture, &c.; the Medical society, established in 1772; the Society for Icelandic literature, founded in 1779; and a board-oflongitude organized in 1784. The Royal library is a very good collection, now consisting of 412,300 volumes. It is principally distinguished for its printed and manuscript editions of the classics, and for an extensive collection of in the Icelandic tongue, written between the 11th and 14th cents. About £1,250 are annually devoted to its support.—The university of C. was founded by Christian I. in 1479, and has been richly endowed by his successors. It consists of 4 colleges, and is generally attended by 1,100 or 1,200 students. The professors, 37 in number, have liberal salaries; and 168 poor scholars are provided with lodgings, firewood, and 3s. a-week. Its library contains about 110,000 volumes, besides 2,000 manuscripts.—About a mile from the city is the national tomb of the gallant men who fell in the conflict of Copenhagen-roads on the 2d of April 1801. It is a pyramidal hillock, planted with sapling poplars, with tomb-stones in front, recording the names of the officers who fell, and their respective ships. It is enclosed with a square palisado; and an obelisk of grey marble, standing on a pedestal of granite, bears the following inscription: "To the memory of those who fell for their country, their grateful fellow-citizens raise this monument. April 2d, 1801." Beneath, on a white marble tablet, under a wreath of laurel, oak, and cypress, is engraven: "The wreath which the country bestows never withers over the grave of the fallen warrior."—The pop. of C. in 1797 was 85.161; in 1825, 108,600; in 1840, 123,000; in 1847, 129,300, of whom 124,000 were Lutheran Protestants. The manufactories of C. are unimportant. It has brandy-distilleries, breweries, sugar-refineries, ironfoundries, soap-works, and tanneries. Its general trade is not considerable. The China trade is a monopoly of the Asiatic company; and the different local trades or crafts are engrossed by guilds or corporations. During the year 1783, there arrived at C. 5,100 ships, besides 5 from China, 9 from India, and 127 from America; and in 1805 the arrivals and departures were as follows: ARRIVALS. Ships. From India and China, . . . From America, Total, DEPARTURES. For the national ports, 3.975 For foreign ports, . 1,720 Total, 5,695 In 1849, exclusive of coasters and fishing-vessels, the number of vessels which arrived in the port of C. was <math>5,371 = 132,656</math> lasts; of which <math>380 = 29,011</math> COP lasts, were from England. The number which sailed was <math>5,422 = 133,540</math> lasts; of which <math>131 = 7.404</math> lasts went to England. The exports of grain from C. in 1849 amounted to 189,200 casks <math>= 3,062,389</math> lbs. Oil-cake was exported to the extent of 13,738 casks. Cattle and distilled spirits are exported to Iceland, Greenland, and the Dutch West Indies; and cheese and butter to the Hanse-towns, Sweden, and Mechlenberg. Live poultry form a considerable article of exportation from C. to the different ports of the Baltic,-A railway from C. to Roeskilde, 24 m. in length, was opened in 1847; it is proposed to carry it forward to Corsoer, 60 m .- The principal domestic trade of C. is with Norway, Iceland, and the Faroe isles. From the former it draws all its cannon, shot, anchors, and iron-work; Russia supplies it with flax, hemp, and masts, and also with some sailcloth and cordage; Sweden with pitch and tar, and Germany with oak. The importations from England are chiefly confined to coals, salt, and iron, for which returns are made in grain, rape-seed, oil-cake, hides, bones, and salted and smoked beef. See articles DENMARK and Sound. The bailiwick of C. comprises an area of 467 sq. m., and had a pop., exclusive of that of the cap., of 69,300 in 1847. It comprises 6 harden, viz.: Sokkelund, comprising the islands of Amager and Saltholm; Smörum; Somme, comprising the town of Roeskilde; Thune, Ramsöe, and Voldborg. C. owes its origin as a city to a castle which was built here in 1160 by Archbishop Wide, to defend the coast against the pirates which then swarmed in the Baltic. Roeskilde was then the cap. of the kingdom; but the protection which the castle afforded, and the convenience of the situation as a port, soon induced many of the islanders to prefer it for their residence; and it gradually increased in size and pop., until it became the seat of the court in 1443, during the reign of Christopher of Bavaria. Since that time, C. may be said to have been more than once entirely rebuilt, as there is perhaps not a house in it 200 years old, and the greater part of them indeed are scarcely above 50. This has been occasioned by the dreadful conflagrations to which it has been frequently exposed, and to which must be attributed its present modern and regular appearance. The fire of 1728, in 48 hours swept away the most elegant part of the city; and 67 streets, containing 1,650 dwelling-houses, 4 churches, the university, and several other public edifices, fell victims to its fury. In 1794, between 900 and 1,000 buildings, including the church of St. Nicholas, and the royal palace of Christianburg, were completely destroyed by a similar calamity; and scarcely were the streets rebuilt in the following year, when another part of the city was reduced to ashes. C. also suffered severely in 1801, when Nelson burnt or sank all the Danish vessels here; and from the bombardment of a British force in 1807, by which the cathedral, with 305 houses, were destroyed, and about 600 houses damaged. It is to these calamities, however, that C. owes its present state of beauty and regularity; and, in proportion to its extent, it contains fewer houses that can be called mean, than any town in the world. Authorities.] M'Donald's Travels through Denmark and part of Sweden in 1809.—Coxe's Travels into Poland, Russia, &c., vol. ii. —Carr's Northern Summer.—Catteau, Tableau des Etats Danois.— Anderson's Tour in Zealand in 1802.—Catteau.—Calleville, Tableau de la Mer Baltique. Paris, 1812.—Feldborg's Denmark.— Conway's [Inglis's] Personal Narrative. Edin., 1829.—Baagesen, Der dänische Staat. Kopen., 1845, 2 vols. 8vo.
COPENHAGEN, a village of Denmark township, Lewis co., in the state of New York, U. S., 149 m. NW of Albany, on Deer river, half-a-mile above a fine fall of that river. Pop. in 1840, 250.

COPERTINO, a town of Naples, in the prov. of the Terra - d'Otranto, district and 11 m. SSW of Lecce, on a hill. It is surrounded by walls, and defended by a strong citadel, and contains several Pop. 645.
COPGROVE, a parish of the W. R. of Yorkshire, 4 m. WSW of Borough-bridge. Area 1,050 acres. Pop. 103.

COPIAPO, the most northerly province of the republic of Chili; separated on the N by the desert of Atacama from Peru; and from La Plata on the E by the cordillera of the Andes, which is here traversed by 5 mountain-passes, distant from each other about [ 20 leagues. The prov. of Coquimbo bounds it on the S; and the Pacific washes its E shores. Its extent is about 250 m. from N to S. From the littoral district lying along the sea, the country rises suddenly to from 50 to 70 ft.; and at that elevation forms a nearly level plain, which is bounded by Monte-Algaroba on the E. To the E of the latter range, the surface is much higher, and more rugged. The climate of C. is healthy, but little or no rain falls throughout its confines; heavy dews and numerous small rivers compensate, however, in a great measure, for this want. No part of Chili is richer in minerals, particularly gold and copper. When Dr. Meyen visited this prov. in 1831, 3 mines of gold, 24 of silver, and 75 of copper, were wrought in the partido of C.; but the richest silver-mines, about 20 m. S of the city of C., were not discovered till 1832. At present great activity prevails in the silver-workings in this prov.; want of hands and of capital, and scarcity of mule-hire, it is said, constitute the only limits to the productiveness of these mines. Copper-foundries are increasing along the coast of this prov. Among its mineral treasures are turquoises and other gems, as well as fossil salt, sulphur, and lapis lazuli.—The principal rivers in this prov. are the Copiapo, Castagno, Chollai, Salada, Totoral, Quebradafronda, and Huasco; the latter, having a port of the same name, with an increasing village, in S lat. 28° 27'. In most maps, on the S boundary of this prov. is inserted the Volcano-del-Copiapo; but no volcano exists between that of Coquimbo under the 30th parallel of S lat., and that of Atacama nearly 8° further N.

COPIAPO, the chief town of the above prov., is situated on a river of the same name, which does not reach the sea, as represented in many maps, but is lost in salt pools, about 20 m. from the port of Caldera, which is in 27° 20' 00" S lat., and 71° 01' 45" W long. [Beechey.] The city is 25 m. direct distance from the Pacific. In 1819 and 1822 it was nearly destroyed by successive earthquakes; but was speedily rebuilt, and in 1831 contained 4,000 inhabitants.—A company has been organized, with a capital of 800,000 dollars, for the construction of a railway from this town to Caldera. Its length will be 48 m., with a gradual ascent of 1,075 ft. to the summit-level, and one short inclined plane.

COPINSHAY, or COPENSAY, one of the Orkney islands, in the parish of Deerness, in N lat. 58° 55', W long. 2° 26'. Pop. in 1831, 7. It is about 1 m. in length, and a n. in breadth, and is chiefly resorted to for the wild-fowl with which it abounds.
COPLE, a parish of Bedfordshire, 32 m. ESE of Bedford, on a branch of the Ouse. Area 2,170 acres. Pop. 551.
COPLESTONE, a hamlet in the p. of Colebrook, Devon, supposed to be of great antiquity.
COPLESTONE (Mount), one of the extreme W summits of the Rocky mountains, on the shore of the Arctic ocean, in N lat. 70° 5', W long. 145° 26'.

COPMANTHORPE, a chapelry in the p. of St. Mary, Bishopshill-Junior, 4 m. SSW, and by railway 3 m. 54 chains from York. Area 1,610 acres. Pop. 284. Copmanthorpe-moor, in this chapelry, is intersected by the York and Derby railway.
COPNOR, a hamlet in the p. of Portsea, Hants. Pop. 141.

COPO'CABA'NA, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Rio-de-Janeiro, parish of São-João-da-Lagoa-de- Freitas. The church is situated on a sand bank called by the Indians Sacopenopan, which is insulated at high tides. A fortress was built in the vicinity, for the defence of the mountain-pass which lies between the sea and the bay of Botafogos, but has been suffered to fall into ruins. COP

COPORORO, a river of South Guinea, in the kingdom of Benguela, which takes its rise in the Serra Fria; unites with the Calunga; and falls into the Atlantic on the N side of Elephant bay, and 35 m. SW of San-Philip-de-Benguela.
COPPA-DROOG, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Mysore, on a mountain near the r. bank of the Toonga, 28 m. SE of Bednore, and 120 m. NW of Seringapatam.
COPPARO, a town of the Papal states, in the legation and 11 m. ENE of Ferrara. Pop. 2,330. It has an annual fair.
COPPENBRUGGE, a town of Hanover, in the principality of Kalenberg, the cap. of the co. of Spiegelberg, 18 m. SSW of Hanover. Pop. 944. It has a castle, and possesses some linen manufactures.

COPPENHALL, a parish of Cheshire, 5 m. NE of Nantwich, and 55 m. from Birmingham, intersected by the Birmingham Grand Junction railway. It consists of the two adjoining townships of Coppenhall-Church and Coppenhall-Monks. Area of the former 1,750 acres. Pop. 544. Area of the latter 1,300 acres. Pop. 203. Total area of p. 3,050 acres. Pop. 747.—Also a chapelry in the p. and 4 m. NNW of Penkridge, Staffordshire, near the Birmingham and Liverpool railway. Area 2,040 acres. Pop. 119.

COPPER, or Copperas Harbour, an inlet on the N side and near the extremity of Point Keewaiwona, on Lake Superior, in the Chippeway territory, in N lat. 47° 28', W long. 88° 4'. It is defended by two islets. The copper-mining speculations now carrying on in this district have given origin to a log-cabin city on the shore of this inlet, which has at present a pop. of some 50 "geologists, explorers, and locationspeculators." [Lanman.] A remarkable vein of black oxide of copper was formerly wrought here, but was abandoned after some £40,000 or £50,000 worth of the ore had been raised. It was the only known locality of this substance, which is a pure oxide, and must not be confounded with what is called 'copperblock,' which occurs in an earthy, pulverulent form, being a mixture of various hydrated oxides. [Silliman's Journal, No. 23.]

COPPERMINE, a large river of N America, falling into the Arctic ocean. It has its source in Lake Providence, in N lat. 65° 50', W long. 112° 30'. From this it flows NNE 20 m., and then WNW, dilating into a lake, or rather series of lakes, called Point Lake, which average 2 m. in width. The source of another principal branch of the C. is in N lat. 65° 12' 40", W long. 113° 8' 25". The united stream is about 100 yards broad, downwards to the lake of the Red Rock, where it receives another tributary from the SW, and below which it gradually augments in size, descending with a rapid current, and in a course nearly direct N, to the parallel of 66° 30'. The scenery on the adjacent shores, and to the W of this section of the river, is very picturesque during the brief summer of these regions; but the country along the E is barren. Between the parallel of 66° 30' and 66° 35' the river breaks through a branch of the Rocky mountains, and its course, hitherto about NNW, turns to the E of N. It now becomes full of rapids, more contracted, and is often pent between perpendicular rocky walls, which rise in some places to 150 ft. The C. mountains extend from the I. bank of the river in lat. 67° 15'. The ore found among the earth and debris in their ravines has given its name both to the stream and to the mountains. No trees are found beyond the parallel of 67° 30'. At the mouth of the river, in N lat. 67° 47' 50", W long. 115° 36′ 49″, the C. is nearly 1 m. broad, but very shallow and full of sand-bars, and is divided into two channels by an island.—A low ridge of greenstone exists at the mouth of the river, and from thence to Bloody-fall, a distance of 10 m., the country is nearly level, with the exception of some low ridges of trap which run through it, and the channel of the river is sunk 150 ft. below the surrounding country. Hearne reached the mouth of this river on the 17th of July, 1771, and ascertained—what till then had been denied—that the shores of America were washed by the ocean. His observations fixed this point in N lat. 71° 54', W long. 120° 30'; but Captain Franklin, in 1823, ascertained it to be N lat. 67° 47', W long. 115° 36'. Franklin's first expedition traced the N coast of America eastward from the mouth of the C. river to 109° 50', W long.; the second traced the coast westward to Point Beechey, in N lat. 70° 24', W long. 149° 37'. Captain Franklin found the rise of the tide here only 4 inches; the variation of the magnetic needle was observed to be 46° 52" E; and its dip 88° 5′ 7". Richardson's river falls into the sea immediately W of the mouth of the C. The whole length of the C. is about 350 m. from the head of Point Lake. White fish and a species of salmon are found in it in abundance.

COPPERMINE RIVER, or Onbonagon, a river of the United States, rising about the parallel of 46°, and flowing N, through the Chippeway territory, into Lake Superior, between Flint-steel river on the E, and Iron river on the W. It is 50 yards wide at its mouth, and has 6 fath. water on its bar.

COPPER MOUNTAINS, a ridge in Arctic America, under the parallel of 67° 15' N lat., rising 800 or 900 ft. above the bed of the Coppermine river. It has a direction from WNW to ESE. Many of the ridges have precipitous sides; and their summits, which are uneven and stony, do not rise more than 200 or 250 ft. above their valleys, which are generally swampy and full of small lakes.

COPPET, or COPET, a parish and town of Switzerland, in the cant. de Vaud, district and 5 m. SSW of Nyon, and 9 m. NNE of Geneva, on the W bank of the lake of Geneva. Pop., Protestant, 467. It contains a fine castle belonging to the lords of Coppet, and noted as the burying place of Neckar and Madame de Staël.
COPPINGFORD, a parish of Huntingdonshire, 6 m. S of Stilton. Area 1,030 acres. Pop. 45.
COPPITO, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Abruzzo-Ultra, 3 m. NW of Aquila, on the r. bank of the Aterno. Pop. 675.
COPPULL, a chapelry in the p. of Standish, Lancashire, 42 m. SSW of Chorley, near the Wigan and Preston railway. Area 2,180 acres. Pop. 1,031.
COPSTON-MAGNA, a hamlet in the p. of Monks- Kirby, Warwickshire, 63 m. ESE of Nuneaton. Pop. 113,

COPTS, a name given to the natives of Egypt, the Christian descendants of the ancient Egyptians, who had intermarried, however, in numerous instances, with Greeks, Nubians, Abyssinians, and other foreigners. The term, of Arabic formation, is supposed by some to be a corruption of the Greek word Aiyua- 7105, converted by the Arabs into Kubti, or Kibti, prenounced Gubti, or Gybti, by the Egyptians. Others trace it to Coptos, a city of Upper Egypt, whither, during the Roman persecutions, many of the Egyptian Christians retired. The Jacobites or Monophysites—who were of Egyptian blood, and far more numerous than their adversaries the Melkites, who were Greeks in faith as well as origin-having been persecuted as heretics by the Greek emperor, were willing, on the Arab invasion of the country, to submit to the arms of Amru-Ibn-el-âás, the Arabian commander, who granted to them immunities which they had not previously possessed, and protected their church from the encroachments of the Constantinopolitan see. But the C. soon found that their privileges would be of little avail under oppressive or fanatical princes. Their wealth, numbers, and respectability rapidly declined; and, though rarely intermarrying with their conquerors, and preserving their features, manners, and religion unaltered, they soon lost their language, which had resisted the influence of the Grecian court for so many ages. In person and features they differ from the other natives of Egypt, showing that they are evidently a distinct race, -an intermediate link in the chain which connects the Negro with the fairer tribes to the N and S of the tropics, and strongly resembling the Abyssinians, who, though extremely dark, are paler than the genuine Negroes. Dark eyes, aquiline noses, and curled hair, are the usual characteristics of both nations; and the mummies which have been examined show the resemblance of the modern Copts to their ancestors. [Blumenbach, in Comment. Reg. Soc. Gottingen, xiv. 38.] Baron Larrey considers one physical type as common to the Copts, the Barabra or Berebers, and the Abyssinians, but he separates this by a broad line from the Negro type on the one hand, and the Arabian on the other. He says the C. have "a yellow dusky complexion, like that of the Abyssinians. Their countenance is full, without being puffed; their eyes are beautiful, clear, almond-shaped, languishing; their cheek-bones are projecting; their noses nearly straight, rounded at the point; their nostrils dilated; the mouth is of moderate size, but the lips are thick; their teeth are white, regular, and scarcely projecting; their beard and hair, black and crisp." Bowring, in his report on the commercial affairs of Egypt, says, "The influence of the Copts is undoubtedly an increasing influence; and they will probably occupy no small part of the field in the future history of Egypt. Theirs have been centuries of cruel sufferings, persecutions, and humiliations. In the eyes of the Turks they have always been the pariahs of the Egyptian people; yet they are an amicable, pacific, and intelligent race, whose worst vices have grown out of their seeking shelter from wrong and robbery. A certain sympathy,—perhaps the result of common sufferings,— exists between the C. and the Arabs. They are the surveyors, the scribes, the arithmeticians, the measurers, the clerks; in a word, the learned men of the land They are to the countinghouse and the pen what the fellah is to the field and the plough. The C. race appears nearly stationary as to numbers; I have reason to believe that in some of the agricultural districts they sometimes adopt the Mussulman creed, though that subjects them to the conscription, from which as Christians they are free. There are 12 Episcopal districts; and the C. bishops elect the patriarch, who exercises not only an ecclesiastical, but frequently a sort of judicial authority among them. Most of the bishops are educated at the convent of St. Anthony, in the desert, where there are about 200 monks, from among whom the patriarch is also generally chosen. The patriarch," Mr. B. adds, "informed me that he calculated the number of C. at about 150,000: I conceive this is too low an estimate. A great many of them are employed in the public offices; their average instruction is far superior to that of the Mussulmans, but between them and the European settlers there is scarcely any intercourse, and as little is known of their domestic habits as of those of the Mahomedans. Their females are equally secluded, and they have their harems like other Orientals. In the remoter parts of Egypt they practise polygamy, and circumcise their children. They occupy a particular quarter in Cairo; few of them are opulent, few engaged in commerce on a large scale. They have many schools in which elementary instruction is communicated, but none where the higher branches of knowledge are taught. Intoxication is a frequent vice amongst the C. In the manufactories of the pasha, many are employed as handloom weavers. The C. profess to be exclusively the descendants of the ancient Egyptians, and consider the rural agricultural population, who are almost wholly Mussulmans, as an inferior race; but there is no sufficient reason for believing that the Christian C. alone are of the old Egyptian family. Though education among them is more diffused than among the Arab race, their reputation for probity and veracity is very low. In the rural districts the habits of the C. are scarcely distinguishable from those of the Arabs. The C. have an era of their own. The present year [1838], which is the 1254th of the Mahomedan Hegira, is the 1555th of the Coptic dates. Persecution of the C. by the Mussulmans has almost entirely ceased. They marry early in life, their girls generally at 12 years of age. The same reserve is practised among them as among the Mussulmans: in fact there is little difference in the domestic manners of the two classes. Divorce is frequent, and easily obtained; indeed, divorce and the conditions of it are generally stipulated for in the marriage-contract. They adopt with the Mussulman all the superstitions of the country, whether the superstition be of Mahomedan or Christian origin. It was a Christian at Minieh who assured me that a certain miraculous column in the mosque there ran down with perspiration every Friday. The Mussulmans are less prompt to credit Christian superstitions than are the C. to adopt those of the Mussulmans." Mr. St. John was informed that the C. pop. of Egypt amounted to 160,000; Lane estimates them at 150,000. They employ the ancient Egyptian or Coptic language in their religious services, but it is translated into Arabic for the benefit of the laity. Their alphabetical characters,—which with a very few exceptions were borrowed from the Greek, and probably first introduced towards the latter end of the 3d cent.,—contributed to preserve their language in its original form; while a desire of instructing the people had led the monks to compose many religious works in their vernacular tongue; but the poverty and ignorance which soon sprung up from the oppression under which they laboured, could not fail to cut them off from the use of such instructors, and accustom them to neglect a language which served only as an invidious distinction. In the lower or N provs. of Egypt, the Coptic appears to have been little if at all spoken in the 10th cent., though used and studied as a learned language as late as the 15th cent. In the Sâid, or Upper Egypt, which was less exposed to foreign influence, it prevailed much longer; and the peculiar dialect of that country was generally spoken by the people in the beginning of the 16th cent. Vansleb, who was there in the latter part of the 17th cent., saw the last of the C. to whom this language could be said to be vernacular. It is an original tongue, having no distinct affinity with any other; though many Greek words have been introduced, unaltered, by Christian writers, and several terms appear to have been anciently borrowed from the Hebrew. The Coptic version of the New Testament is valuable on account of its antiquity, dating, according to several critics, as early as the 2d cent., and not later than the 5th at the lowest computation. As a relic of the ancient Egyptian, also, the C. language is deserving of attention; and the light which a study of the fragments written in it will throw on the history and antiquities of ancient Egypt has been shown in the works of M. Quatremère and M. Champollion.

COPUL, a fortified town of Hindostan, in the prov. and 110 m. SSE of Bejapore, on a mountain near an affluent of the Tungabudra, in N lat. 15° 20', E long. 76° 12' 30". It was formerly considered one of the strongest fortresses in India, and in 1790 held out against the Nizam army for six months.

COQ (LE), or Braknas, a town of Senegambia, in the kingdom of Futa-Toro, on the S bank of the Senegal, at the W extremity of an island formed by an inosculation of that river, and a little to the SW of Podor or Fort George. Pop. 5,000, chiefly Moors. -About 5 m. NW is the Escale du Coq or Braknas, called also the Escale de Donaye, which forms the great entrepot for gums from the forests on the N side of the Senegal. See Braknas.

COQUEIROS, an island of Brazil, in the prov. and 8 m. N of Rio-de-Janeiro, and parish of the ilha do Governador.-Also a town of the prov. of Sergipe, on the shore of the Atlantic, at the mouth of the Irapirany or Vaza-Barris.
COQUET. See COCKET.

COQUILLE, or AIGNAY, a small river of France, in the dep. of the Côte-d'Or, which takes its rise between Mortot and Chandarcon, and after a course of about 9 m. falls into the r. side of the Revinson, near the village of Taperon.
COQUILLE (LA), a hamlet of France, in the dep. of the Dordogne, cant. of Jumillac-le-Grand, com. of Sainte-Marie-de-Frugie. Pop. 180.

COQUIMBO, a department of Chili, bounded on the N by Copiapo; on the E by the Andes; on the SE by Aconcagua; on the SW by Quillota; and on the W by the Pacific. It is 120 m. in length, and 110 m. in breadth. Area 31,899 sq. m. [Weimar Almanach]. Estimated pop. 30,000. Its rivers are the Coquimbo, Tongoi, Limari, and Chuapa. The surface is for the most part broken and unwatered. Rain, indeed, sometimes does not fall for two or three years. The coast exhibits remarkable proofs of successive changes in the relative level of sea and land, in a series of five narrow, gently sloping terraces, which rise one behind the other, and are formed of shingle, and covered with sea-shells. [Darwin.] These terraces front the bay of C. and sweep up the valley. They are finely described by Captain Hall in the 2d vol. of his Extracts from a Journal. The soil produces, though not in abundance, grain, wine, olives, and oil of excellent quality. There are both in the Cordilleras, and in the hills towards the sea, many gold mines, likewise some of silver, copper, lead, sulphur, white lime, and salt; but the metal most abundantly produced is copper, large quantities of which are now exported.

COQUIMBO, or LA SERENA, the capital of the above prov., is situated near the mouth and on the S side of a river of the same name, sometimes called the Chuapa, 7 m. distant from the sea, upon a range of elevated ground from which there is a delightful prospect. This city, which is in S lat. 29° 54′ 10″, W long. 71° 18' 45" [Beechey], 270 m. NNW of Santiago, was founded in 1544. It is of considerable size, and regularly built. It contained, at the beginning of the cent., 500 families of Spaniards and people of colour, with some Indians. Captain Andrews estimated the COR pop. at 6,000 in 1825-6. In 1838 it is said to have contained 10,000. The streets are built in straight lines, and the houses are disjoined from each other by large gardens, which are well supplied with water by a canal from the river, which is brought into the town, and subdivided into lesser channels that convey it to the houses and gardens. Besides the parish-church, C. contains several convents, and a college formerly belonging to the Jesuits. The city is healthy, and enjoys a great uniformity of temp. throughout the year, hence the name it received from the old Spanish inhabitants La Serena. The country around the city, except where irrigated, is an arid sandy desert, covered with loose sand of the new red sandstone series. In the vicinity, the rocks are of a transition granite, with masses of porphyry in veins. The intervening space betwixt C. and its port is a marsh from which the sea has retired, and only wants draining to render it extremely valuable. Its port, in S lat. 29° 55′ 10″, W long. 71° 25′ 10″, is little more than a collection of huts, with a custom-house, situated at the head of a small indentation of the coast open to the N. There is sufficient depth of water here for large vessels; but the port has only one narrow entrance, and a rough short sea is often experienced here [Blanckley]: besides no fresh water can be procured, and supplies of all kinds, except shell-fish, are scarce and dear. Supplies are chiefly obtained from Santiago or Valparaiso. From 500,000 to 550,000 dollars, besides gold, are annually exported from C. to Europe; but the duty on this article being very heavy, it is frequently smuggled on board, consequently no estimate of the quantity shipped can be formed. In 1834, 83,979 marcs of silver, and 42,860 quintals of copper were exported from this place; in 1839, 15,087 marcs of native sulphur, 29,457 cwt. of copper, and 23,053 cwt. of copper ore. C. was repeatedly taken and plundered during the 16th and 17th cent.
COQUIN SOUND, an indentation of the W coast of Greenland, to the N of Baal's river.

CORA, or Cori, a town of the Papal states, in the deleg. of Frosinone, and 9 m. ESE of Velletri, on the Volscian ridge. Its streets are closely huddled together, and climb steeply up the declivity. Pop. 3,000. It is a place of high antiquity, and there are numerous ancient remains here.

CORAÇÃO-DE-JESUS, a parish of Brazil, in the prov. of Minas-Geraes, situated in the Serra-de- Hucambira, 32 m. NE of Formiga. The rearing of cattle forms the only industrial employment of the inhabitants, who are of the poorest description.

CORACE, a river of Naples, which takes its rise in the Apennines near the S confines of the prov. of Calabria-Citra, runs through the N part of Calabria- Ultra, and falls into the gulf of Squillace, at Catanzaro, 6 m. NE of Squillace, after a total course SSE of about 30 m.
CORACHIE. See KURACHI.
CORAGIAS, a tribe of South American Indians who inhabit the banks of the Araguaya and Tocantins, in the prov. of Goyaz, Brazil.
CORAH. See KORAH.
CORAL, a small rocky island of Brazil, near the coast of the prov. of São-Paulo, to the SE of the bay of Paranagua, in S lat. 25° 45′ 49".
CORAM, a village of Brookhaven township, Suffolk co., in the state of New York, U. S., 203 m. SSE of Albany.

CORANSAH, or CORANZA, a kingdom of Upper Guinea, on the Gold coast, bounded on the N by the kingdom of Banna, on the E by that of Gobati, on the SE by the kingdom of Ajorah, on the SW by the kingdom of Ashantee, to which it is tributary, and on the NW by that of Takima. It is about 135 m. in length, from NE to SW; and its greatest breadth is 60 m. The inhabitants belong to the same stock as the Ashantees, but are superior to that people in intelligence and civilization. The cap., of the same name, is 60 m. NE of Coomassie.
CORAX (Mount), or VEDRIAITZA, a mountainrange of Greece, diverging from Parnassus, and running S towards Lepanto.
CORAPAS, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Matto- Grosso, on the l. bank of the Canastra, 80 m. NE of Fort-Principe-de-Beira.

CORATO, a town of Naples, cap. of a circondario, in the prov. of the Terra-di-Bari, district and 15 m. SSE of Barletta. Pop. 13,163. It contains a handsome church, several convents, and an orphan's asylum.
CORAY, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Finistère, cant. of Châteauneuf-du-Faon, 18 m. SE of Chateaulin. Pop. 1,640.

CORAZON (EL), a summit of the Andes, in the republic of Ecuador, prov. of Pichincha, 25 m. SSW of Quito, in S lat. 0° 34' 18". It has an alt. of 15,795 ft., and gives origin to some of the head-streams of the Esmeraldas. It derives its name from the heartshape of its outline.

CORBACH, or Korbach, a town, cap. of the principality and 10 m. NW of Waldeck, and 30 m. W of Cassel, on the Itter, by which the new is separated from the old town. Pop. 2,200. It contains a eastle, two churches, a gymnasium, and an orphan's asylum, and has some manufactures of woollen fabrics.
CORBALLIS, a village in the p. of Duleek, co. Meath, 33 m. S of Drogheda. Pop. in 1831, 127.

CORBALLY, a parish in co. Cork, 54 m. SW of Cork. Area 869 acres. Pop. 193.—Also a parish, partly in co. Leinster, but chiefly in co. Tipperary, in the vicinity of Roserea and Borris-in-Ossory. Area of the Tipperary section, 11,879 acres. Pop. of the whole, in 1831, 3,090; in 1841, 3,373.—Also a parish in co. Waterford, 63 m. SSE of Waterford. Area 725 acres. Pop. 315.—Also a village in the p. of Castle-Connor, co. Sligo. Pop. 167.
CORBARA, a commune and beautiful village of the island of Corsica, eant. of L'Ile Rousse, 9 m. ENE of Calvi. Pop. 1,144. Along the coast here are fine granite quarries.
CORBEAU, a village of Champlain township, Clinton co., in the state of New York, U. S.

CORBEIL, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Seine-et-Oise. The arrond., comprising an area of 60,004 hectares, contains 4 cant., viz.: Arpajon, Boissy-Saint-Léger, Corbeil, and Longjumeau. Pop. in 1831, 56,733; in 1846, 60,198. The cant. comprises 26 com. Pop. in 1831, 16,297; in 1841, 18,523. The town is situated on the l. bank of the Seine, at the confluence of the Essonne, and opposite Vieux-Corbeil, at an alt. of 147 ft. above sea-level, 21 m. SSE of Paris, and 24 m. SE of Versailles. Pop. in 1789, 2,522; in 1821, 3,481; in 1836, 3,690; and in 1846, 4,455. It has several churches, a library, a theatre, &c., but derives its chief importance from its trade and manufacturing industry. The former consists chiefly in grain and flour, for which it is the principal entrepot for the Paris market. Sugar, leather, chintzes, calico, cashmeres, and silk hats, form the chief articles of local manufacture. On the banks of the rivers are numerous flour-mills. Fairs are held twice a-year, and two markets weekly. Regular communication with Paris is maintained by railroad and steam-boats.—C. was originally a place of considerable strength. In 1418 it was unsuccessfully besieged by the Duke of Burgundy, and again by the Calvinists in 1562. In 1590, after holding out for a month, it was taken by the Duke of Parma and devastated by fire and sword.
CORBEILLES, a commune and village of France, COR in the dep. of Loiret, cant. of Ferrières, 9 m. NW of Montargis. Saffron is extensively cultivated here.

CORBELIN, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Nievre, cant. of Varzy, on a stream of the same name which flows from the etang of C. to the Sosay.—Also a commune in the dep. of Isère, cant. of Pont-de- Beauvoisin, 5 m. NE of La Tour-du-Pin. Pop. 1,801.
CORBENAY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Haute-Saone, cant. of Saint-Loup. Pop. 1,007.
CORBENY, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Aisne, cant. of Craonne, 10 m. SE of Laon. Pop. 925.
CORBE'RE, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Pyrenees-Orientales, cant. of Millas, 12 m. WSW of Perpignan. Pop. 1,361.
CORBETTA, a town of Venetian-Lombardy, in the gov. of Milan, deleg. and 20 m. NW of Pavia. Pop. 3,500.

CORBIE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Somme, arrond. of Amiens. The cant., comprising 24 com., had a pop. of 21,300 in 1841.— The town is situated on the r. bank of the Somme, 8 m. E of Amiens, on the Great Northern railway from Paris to the coast, by which it is 163 kilom. distant from Paris, and 78 kilom. from Donai. Pop. 2,745. It is a place of some manufacturing industry; and was at one time a strongly fortified town.
CORBIERES, or Korbers, a parish and village of Switzerland, in the cant. and 8 m. S of Friburg, near the r. bank of the Sarime, and at an alt. of 2,352 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 193, Protestants.

CORBIE'RES (LES), a mountain-range in France, in the deps. of Aude and Pyrenees-Orientales, a contrefort of the Pyrenees, separating the basin of the Aude from that of the Agly and the Tet. Its culminating point is the Roc Blanc, near Querigut. The Montagne d'Alaric and the Carcassone hills are spurs of the C.

CORBIGNY, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Nièvre, arrond. of Clamecy.— The cant. comprises 15 com. Pop. 12,369.—The town is on the I. bank of the Anguison, near its confluence with the Yonne, 18 m. SSE of Clamecy. Pop. 2,124.
CORBON, a village of France, in the dep. of Orne, cant. and 6 m. SE of Mortagne. Pop. 324. It gave name to the ancient district of Corbonnais.
CORBONES, a river of Spain, in the prov. of Seville, rising in the sierra 12 m. NNE of Ronda, and flowing to the Guadalquivir, which it joins on the l. bank, after a course of 45 m. from SSE to NNW.
CORBONOD, a village of France, in the dep. of Ain, cant. and 3 m. NW of Seyssel. Pop. 1,382.

CORBRIDGE, a parish and village in Northumberland, on the N bank of the Tyne, 41 m. E of Hexham, intersected by the Newcastle and Carlisle railway, which has a station here 17 m. from Newcastle, and 424 m. from Carlisle. Area of p. 13,130 acres. Pop. in 1801, 1,744; in 1841, 2,103. Large fairs for the sale of live stock are held here.
CORBY, a parish in Lincolnshire, 33 m. S of Lincoln, on the river Glen. Area 3,790 acres. Pop. 714.—Also a p. in Northamptonshire, 3 m. SE of Rockingham. Area 2,800 acres. Pop. 791.

CORBY (GREAT and LITTLE), two townships in Cumberland; the former in the p. of Wetherall, <math>\tilde{o}_{4}^{3}</math> m. ESE of Carlisle; the other in the p. of Warwick, 53 m. ENE of Carlisle. Area of Great C. 2,960 acres. Pop. 806. Area of Little C. 380 acres. Pop. 283. C. castle, the seat of the Howard family, in Great C., is a noble and ancient edifice, finely situated on a cliff rising above the Eden.

CORCHUELAS (LAS), a town of Spain, in Estremadura, in the prov. and 30 m. NE of Caceres, near the l, bank of the Tagus. France, in the dep. of Vosges, arrond. of Saint-Dié. —The cant. comprises 13 com. Pop. 11,947.—The com., 8 m. S of Saint-Dié, has a pop. of 1,558.
CORCLONE, a parish in Queen's co., 24 m, NE of Stradbally. Area 3,644 acres. Pop. 749.
CORCOLES, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 36 m. ESE of Guadalaxara, near the r. bank of the Guadiela.
CORCOMOHIDE, a parish in cos. Cork and Limerick, 9 m. S of Rathkeale. Area of Cork section 440 acres; of Limerick section 9,573 acres. Pop. of the whole 5,943.
CORCORA, a town of Abessinia, in the prov. of Wojerat, 65 m. SSE of Antalo.

CORCOVADO, a beautiful conical mountain of Brazil, rising above the city of Rio-de-Janeiro, on the N side of the valley of Laranjeiras, to an alt. of 2,806 ft. [Beechey.] The city, with its suburbs, embraces the N and E sides of the foot of the C., into the very defiles of which the adjacent hamlets rise picturesquely. The summit of the C. consists of two granitic peaks, parted by a narrow ravine. The view from the summit is wonderfully fine, comprising the valley of Laranjeiras, the city, the whole extent of the bay, the distant range of the Orgãos, the Lagoa-de-Rodrigode-Freitas, the placid bay of Bota-Fogo, the Sugarloaf mountain, and the peaks of Gavia and Tijuca. The greater part of the mountain is covered with a dense forest, which varies considerably in character with the alt. Towards the summit large trees become rare; while bamboos and ferns are numerous, and flowering shrubs and parasites extend the whole way. The C. is the great reservoir which supplies the city of Rio with water. The vapours raised from the Atlantic are intercepted by this lofty peak, round which they collect and congregate in the form of dense mists during the greater part of the year, and descend in numerous rills down the sides, particularly on the N or that which faces the city. Here they unite to form a copious cataract, which falls into a natural reservoir, whence they are conducted by a fine aqueduct, constructed by Albuquerque in 1719, to the convent of Santa Theresa, and thence across the valley, by a magnificent arcade completed in 1750, to the hill of San Antonio, whence it descends to the city by a canal 3 m. in length, consisting of two walls 6 ft. high, and arched over, with sufficient pass for workmen to enter it occasionally, and openings at intervals for light and air.—There is a volcanic mountain of the same name in Chiloe, in S lat. 43° 11′ 20″, W long. 72° 48′ 40″, 93 m. from San Carlos. It is often in violent action.

CORCUBION, a small fishing-town of Spain, in Galicia, in the prov. of La Corogne, on a bay of the same name, to the E of Cape Finistère. Pop. 864. The bay affords good shelter from N winds, with regular shoaling from 13 to 2 fath.
CORDE'AC, a commune of France, in the dep. of Isère, cant. of Mens. Pop. 1,081.
CORDELLE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Loire, cant. of Saint-Symphorien-de-Lay, 6 m. S of Roanne. Pop. 1,310.
CORDEMAIS, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Loire-Inferieure, cant. of Saint-Etiennede-Mont-Luc, near the r. bank of the Loire. Pop. 2,738.

CORDES, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Tarn, arrond. of Gaillac .- The cant. comprises 19 com. Pop. 10,174. — The com. and town are 10 m. N of Gaillac, near the l. bank of the Ceron. Pop. 2,779.
CORDES BAY, a bay of Patagonia, in the strait of Magalhaens, 4 m. E of Cape Gallant. At the bottom of the bay is a three-peaked mountain about COR
CORCIEUX, a canton, commune, and town of 1,500 ft. high. The anchorage is good in 5 to 7 fath.

CORDEVOLE, a river of N Italy, which rises in Tyrol, 18 m. SE of Brixen; flows N through the Lombardo-Venetian prov. of Belluno, passing Agordo; and after a course of 48 m., joins the Piave near Meli.
CODILLERA. See ANDES.
